2: In Bangladesh, poultry production and commercialization has grown at an average annual rate of 20% since the early 1990s, despite the bird flu that hit the industry in 2007, 2009 and 2011. The livestock sub-sector contributed 5.8% of GDP in the fiscal year 2010-2011 [1]. Small poultry farms have been a profitable business enhancing the overall rural economy - the average cost benefit ratio of small poultry farms with layer chicken production varies between 1.15 and 1.26 [2,3]. Farms are now using improved ge- netics, feeds and management practices to increase productivity and profitability [4,5]. But, despite many improvements and ben- efits to the rural economy, poultry farms also cause severe envi- ronmental problems viz. greenhouse gas emissions, and water and air pollution [6,7]. In fact, agriculture is the major contributor (75 MtCOe) of GHG emissions in Bangladesh, or 39% of all emissions [8].
Approximately 22000 tons of litter is produced each day from 116000 poultry farms [9]. Conventionally, this litter is dumped in

low ground neighboring areas [10]. The proper management of these poultry farms is important not only for food security and reduction of malnutrition (SDG 2) but also for climate change mitigation (SDG 13). Lowering carbon emissions from waste by using waste-to-fuel technologies is among the priorities of Ban- gladesh's Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDC) and proposed Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMA) [11,12]. A better understanding of the context in which poultry farms operate in the country and the potential benefits that can be accrued can help define specific actions to mitigate their environ- mental impact both globally and locally. The environmental problems associated with poultry farms have been documented in case studies from various countries [7,13-18]. Some studies have analyzed manure management of poultry farms and the use of slurry as a source of nutrients to enrich soils [10,19]. The utilization of the waste for energy recovery from waste, for example, in the form of biogas, improves waste disposal and contributes to reducing GHG emissions [9,20]. More recently, emissions reduction through transition to organic crop, livestock management practices, and use of litter as a fuel instead of using it as fertilizer has been studied [17,18]. However, many of these studies have been conducted in developed countries and modern and well managed livestock farms.

Techno-economic analyses for biogas production from animal waste and its utilization have been carried out in previous studies for Bangladesh [21-24]. Rahman et al. [25] estimated that 63000 anaerobic digestion units of different sizes (ranging from 2 m³ to 150 could be built in the country using poultry litters. Anaer- obic digestion (AD) has been mainly considered as a way to generate renewable energy, and the potential amounts and use of the energy have been assessed, and increasing attention is now being given to environmental benefits such as the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions [26,27]. However, conditions may vary, and thus contextualization is needed when addressing the climate change mitigation potential of different regions and countries. This study attempts to fill this gap with a focus on the context of Bangladesh.

The objective of the paper is to estimate the GHG emissions of a rural poultry farm in Bangladesh considering the local supply chains, and explore pathways to reduce GHG emissions of the poultry activities. We consider the cases when the poultry farm is integrated with (i) an anaerobic digester for producing biogas and, (ii) an electricity generation unit comprising an anaerobic digester and a generator. The scope of the paper is limited to evaluation of environmental benefits from such integration in terms of GHG emissions reduction. The poultry farm studied focuses on layer chicken to produce eggs. This is a typical poultry farm in the country and thus the results are relevant to increase understanding about the climate mitigation potential in the sector.

In the next section, the methodological approach, the system boundaries defined for the analysis, andthe data sources used in the paper are presented. The third section discusses the mass flow and energy flow analysis, and the GHG emissions estimation associated with different modules. Estimation of the overall GHG emissions from the poultry farming system and the sensitivity analysis are presented in section 4, followed by conclusions in section 5.

6: Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been previously used to evaluate poultry production systems either considering the whole supply chain or parts of it [14,16,28-37]. Most of the LCA studies in the poultry sector are focused on the farm itself [28,29,31], though some have also captured the slaughtering stage and/or packaging and distribution of products beyond the farm gate [14,32,38]. Boggia et al. [33] compared various farming systems with different rearing practices (conventional and organic) to identify the best option from an environmental perspective. Lesschen et al. [16] evaluated the environmental profile of the livestock sectors in European countries. The authors found large variation of emissions among the countries, mainly due to differences in livestock pro- duction systems. Katajajuuri [28] and Pelletier [29] made a life cycle assessment of the boiler chicken considering the whole supply chain. They also estimated the associated global warming, eutro- phication and acidification potential related to the production cy- cle, whereas Fiala [39], Leinonen et al. [40] and Skunca et al. [41] analyzed the environmental impact of chicken meat processing units. Most of these studies focused on developed countries with advanced livestock production systems. Fewer efforts have been made to analyze the environmental problems associated with rural poultry farms in developing countries such as Bangladesh.

In this study, we have applied LCA to estimate GHG emissions reduction potential while managing the poultry litter of a poultry farm in Bangladesh. Conventional poultry farming practices have been considered in the analysis as most of the poultry farms in the country adopt conventional rearing. The relevant data for the assessment were collected during field visits (i.e. the poultry farm and feed factory). The case farm used is situated at Elenga village in

Tangail district of Bangladesh. This is a typical size rural poultry farm with 3000 layer chickens, producing 1200 to 1400 eggs per day. About 500 kg of feed is used each day. The farm generates 300 kg of poultry waste per day (see Fig. 1).

There is no poultry feed factory available in Tangail or the nearby district. Thus, the farm brings feed from either "Mymensingh" (75 km from the poultry farm) or "Gazipur" (65 km from the poultry farm) depending on market prices and availability. Mars Feed Ltd, one of the poultry feed factories in "Mymensingh" was visited for collecting data regarding feedstock, energy and mass balance. The factory is situated in "Banarpar" village of "Muktagasa" sub district (see Fig. 2).

8: <header>2.1. Functional unit, allocations and system boundary </header>

The functional unit is a means to understand functionalities of the studied system and provides a standard basis for comparison among different systems or sub systems in a study [27,33]. A poultry farm can produce multiple outputs including edible prod- ucts such as eggs and meat, as well as litter and by-products from slaughter. Since the reference case in this study is the layer chicken farm, meat and by-products from slaughter are not considered. We are comparing the GHG emissions reduction potential resulting from the adoption of various solutions to deal with the litter generated without altering the production of eggs. Thus, the functional unit used is expressed in "kg of carbon dioxide equiva- lents (kgCO2eq) emission associated with the production of 10,000 eggs in the poultry farm".

ISO [42] suggests avoiding allocation by splitting the main process into sub-processes or by expansion of the main product system with additional functions related to co-products. In case the allocation is unavoidable, then physical relationships, mass, energy, exergy, or economic allocation are used in LCA to attribute relative shares of GHG emissions to the various goods and services pro- duced [14,27,43]. González-García et al. [14] suggests avoiding allocation in cases when one of the products has no commercial value. The main output from the poultry farm is edible products in the form of eggs. Thus, we opted to determine emissions in kg of carbon dioxide equivalents associated with 10000 eggs production as the functional unit.

Defining the system boundary is important in LCA. The system defined in this study includes the poultry farm and its supply chain as shown in Fig. 3. In this study, we compare alternatives to manage the litter and reduce GHG emission intensity in the poultry farms without altering the production of edible goods.
Four different modules were analyzed along the supply chain
Fig. 1. Internal view of the poultry (layer chicken) farm at Elenga village in Tangail district, Bangladesh.
Fig. 2. Mars feed Ltd - a poultry feed company in Muktagasa sub-district, Bangladesh.

Fig. 3. System boundary considered in the LCA of a poultry supply chain in Bangladesh. Note: The feed module comprises production of feed/food for the chickens raised on the farm. The farm module covers the activities within the poultry farm premises, where the chickens are raised and eggs are laid. The biogas module is used for managing the litter and producing energy in the farm. The electricity module comprises the biogas engine to generate electricity from biogas.

focusing on management and utilization of litter from the poultry farm for energy provision. These modules consist of a feed module, a farm module, a biogas module and an electricity module. Based upon the poultry litter management conditions and slurry utiliza- tion possibilities, four cases have been defined. Table 1 provides an overview of the modules included in each of the four cases considered.
Energy flow, material flow and emissions have been calculated for each module separately, and then combined to estimate the overall GHG emissions reduction potential in four alternative cases.

Case 1 refers to present conditions in which manure from the poultry farm is not treated, but rather stocked in pits. In Case 2, the poultry farm has an anaerobic digester producing biogas which is used as cooking gas, but the digested slurry is disposed in open field. In Case 3, the poultry farm has an anaerobic digester pro- ducing biogas (to be used as cooking gas) and the digested slurry is utilized as fertilizer. In Case 4, the poultry farm is equipped with a biogas digester along with an engine for generating electricity, and the slurry is utilized as fertilizer. Further details on the various modules are discussed in section 2.2. An overview of mass and energy flows and output products is provided in Fig. 4 and dis- cussed in Section 3.

ISO [42] suggests avoiding allocation by splitting the main process into sub-processes or by expansion of the main product system with additional functions related to co-products. In case the allocation is unavoidable, then physical relationships, mass, energy, exergy, or economic allocation are used in LCA to attribute relative shares of GHG emissions to the various goods and services pro- duced [14,27,43]. González-García et al. [14] suggests avoiding allocation in cases when one of the products has no commercial value. The main output from the poultry farm is edible products in the form of eggs. Thus, we opted to determine emissions in kg of carbon dioxide equivalents associated with 10000 eggs production as the functional unit.

Defining the system boundary is important in LCA. The system defined in this study includes the poultry farm and its supply chain as shown in Fig. 3. In this study, we compare alternatives to manage the litter and reduce GHG emission intensity in the poultry farms without altering the production of edible goods.
Four different modules were analyzed along the supply chain

Fig. 2. Mars feed Ltd - a poultry feed company in Muktagasa sub-district, Bangladesh.

Fig. 3. System boundary considered in the LCA of a poultry supply chain in Bangladesh. Note: The feed module comprises production of feed/food for the chickens raised on the farm. The farm module covers the activities within the poultry farm premises, where the chickens are raised and eggs are laid. The biogas module is used for managing the litter and producing energy in the farm. The electricity module comprises the biogas engine to generate electricity from biogas.

focusing on management and utilization of litter from the poultry farm for energy provision. These modules consist of a feed module, a farm module, a biogas module and an electricity module. Based upon the poultry litter management conditions and slurry utiliza- tion possibilities, four cases have been defined. Table 1 provides an overview of the modules included in each of the four cases considered.
Energy flow, material flow and emissions have been calculated for each module separately, and then combined to estimate the overall GHG emissions reduction potential in four alternative cases.

Case 1 refers to present conditions in which manure from the poultry farm is not treated, but rather stocked in pits. In Case 2, the poultry farm has an anaerobic digester producing biogas which is used as cooking gas, but the digested slurry is disposed in open field. In Case 3, the poultry farm has an anaerobic digester pro- ducing biogas (to be used as cooking gas) and the digested slurry is utilized as fertilizer. In Case 4, the poultry farm is equipped with a biogas digester along with an engine for generating electricity, and the slurry is utilized as fertilizer. Further details on the various modules are discussed in section 2.2. An overview of mass and energy flows and output products is provided in Fig. 4 and dis- cussed in Section 3.

13: The feed module represents production of feed for the chicken raised on the farm. The module encompasses all the energy and material flows in and out of the feed factory. In order to produce chicken feed, a number of raw materials are required which are categorized under the material flow. Poultry diet consists of a mixture of ingredients viz. cereal grains, cereal byproducts, plant protein sources, animal byproducts, vitamin and mineral comple- ments, amino acids [44]. All these raw materials are transported from the field to the poultry feed factory, and the produced poultry feed is distributed to various poultry farms mainly via truck and human labour. The factory also needs electricity to run equipment such as boilers, mixers, cutters etc.

The farm module covers the activities within the poultry pre- mises, where the chickens are raised and taken care of. Energy, fresh water and poultry feed are some of the material in flows whereas eggs are out flow or output product from the farm. The hens start laying eggs at approximately 18 weeks of age and, nor- mally, each hen lays around 150-200 eggs per year. The egg pro- duction period lasts for about 1.5 years. In addition, litter is collected and stocked in an open pit near the farm house. This type of litter management system is the most common in Bangladesh [45].

The biogas module is used for managing and utilizing the litter to produce energy in the farm. The biogas module deals with the energy and material flows required for treating the litter and pro- ducing biogas as well as handling the bio slurry. Water and poultry litter are the inputs to the module. Poultry litter is mixed with water to produce slurry before entering the digester. Added water enhances the digestion rate [46]. The produced biogas can be distributed for cooking purpose or could be used to generate electricity. The digested slurry coming out from the digester can be dumped, stored in a slurry pit, or used as fertilizer in agricultural farms [47]. If the bio slurry is not properly utilized as fertilizer, significant amount of greenhouse gases will be generated [48]. In addition, there might be methane emissions due to leakage of 5% biogas from the digester [49].
The electricity module is a biogas engine to generate electricity.

17: We focus on emissions of three greenhouse gases, namely N20, CH4 and CO2, which are associated with the poultry supply chain. We assume that all the NOx is converted to N2 o unless otherwise indicated. The GHG emission sources and sinks included in the analysis are shown in Table 2 together with sources of emissions that have been excluded from the analysis. The GWP values used are those suggested by the IPCC [50] and refer to 100 years' time horizon.
(a) 1 kg CO2 = 1 kgCO2eq (b) 1 kg CH4 = 25 kgCO2eq (c)1 kg N20 = 298 kgCO2eq

The uncertainty of N20 and CO2 emissions associated with growing crops needed for feed production is high as there is no proper data available on the rates at which synthetic and organic fertilizers are applied, or energy used in field operations and crop processing. Therefore, the emission associated with crop growing is not covered in the study. Similarly, indirect CO2 emission related to land use and infrastructure and the emission associated with pro- duction of utensils and medicine have not been considered in the analysis. These parameters have limited influence on emission in- tensity considering the whole life cycle [27].

19: Fig. 4 illustrates the material and energy flows (inputs and outputs) in the poultry farm and the poultry feed factory where the case study was carried out. The estimation at each stage is based on the average value per day. This comprises production and acquisi- tion of the feed inputs i.e. energy and materials required for pro- ducing poultry feed, diesel and other forms of energy required to transport the raw materials to the feed industry, on-farm transport as well as animal feeding and rearing, and poultry litter manage- ment processes. Transport of small consumable items such as polyethylene netting, detergents, medicine and mineral supple- ments used on the poultry farm have not been taken into account in this study. The energy and mass flow associated with each module
Fig. 4. Mass and energy flow per day in a layer chicken farming system at Elenga village, Bangladesh.
is further discussed in this section.

Poultry feed consists of maize, dry fish, rice, crop residues and small amount of fungicides, soybean oil and others [14]. According to the manager of the feed factory, the factory produces 83.3 tonne of feedstock per day. The major ingredients in their poultry feed are maize (50%), rice crop residues (20%) and dried fish (10%). Water amounts to 20% of the input and is added in the feedstock pro- duction process in the factory.

Maize and fish are transported from different parts of the country in trucks, mostly from Bogra which is the closest district to the feed factory. The rest of the ingredients are collected from the local market of Tangail district via human pulled vans. The largest dried fish market in Bangladesh is in the port city "Chittagong." Hence, for simplification, it is assumed that the total maize is supplied from Bogra and dried fish is supplied from Chittagong. Produced poultry feed is distributed to various parts of the country via trucks. Table 3 shows the key parameters and assumptions for energy and material flows estimation in the feed module.

Each day, 0.5 tonnes of poultry feed is brought to the poultry farm to feed around 3000 layer chickens. Apart from this, 12.3 kWh of electricity is used in the poultry farm per day. This electricity consumption includes 12 electrical fans (50 W each operating on average 6 h/day), 10 light bulbs (60 W each and operating 12 h/day) and 1 water pump (1 Hp running 2 h/day). Besides, around 800 L of water is used in the poultry farm. The water is needed on the farm (i) for providing drinking water to the chicken and (ii) for cleaning and other purposes. The farm produces around 1300 eggs per day

and there is some energy needed to transport these eggs from the farm to the market. Table 4 shows some of the key parameters and assumptions for energy and material flows estimation in the poultry farm. About 300 kg of poultry litter is produced each day (i.e. 100 g per chicken per day) which are collected outside the poultry farm in an open pit. An anaerobic digester could be installed for litter treatment to avoid disposal and pollution problems. In the anaerobic digester, organic nutrient-rich substrate is converted into energy by mi- crobes. The mixing ratio of poultry litter to water influences the biogas production. A previous study has shown that a litter to water ratio of 1:1, 2:1 and 3:1 results in a biogas production of 20.8, 40.3 and 131.9 1/kg of total solid, respectively [51]. We have based our estimation on a ratio of 3:1 litter to water aiming at a high biogas yield. Hence, 400 kg of poultry slurry (i.e. mixture of 300 kg of litter and 100 kg of water) enters the biogas digester each day. The total amount of solids in the poultry litter varies between 25 and 50% [23,52]. We have assumed total solid of 38%. The poultry litter contains high amount of dry matter, which leads to a higher biogas yield compared to cattle or pig manure [53]. The amount of biogas produced can be estimated using the following equation. Biogas Produced (m3/day) = Total solid(%)*ga yield (1) (m3/kg TS) *litter slurry yield(kg/day)

As shown in Table 20 /day biogas (i.e. 0.067 m³ of biogas/kg of poultry litter) can be produced from the poultry farm. This estimation is close to the average biogas production from poultry in Bangladesh (i.e. 0.07 /kg of the litter) estimated by Halder et al. [54]. In an average, 200 layer chickens are needed to meet the cooking energy demand of a rural household in Bangladesh [55]. Therefore, the biogas produced from the reference poultry farm considered here could be used for meeting the cooking demand of approximately 15 households in the nearby vicinity.
Around 0.378 tonne of digested slurry comes out from the digester every day. In most biogas plants in Bangladesh, digested

slurry is thrown outside the farm. In some cases, however, the digested slurry is used as fertilizer. This paper has analyzed GHG emissions considering both cases (see next section). A generator unit can be installed along with the biogas digester to generate electricity. Assuming 5% of biogas leakage in the system, biogas engine efficiency of 33%, and calorific value of 1 m³ biogas at 22 MJ, the system can produce 38 kWh electricity per day. The key parameters and assumptions are listed in Table 6.

29: Emissions from the feed module are mainly associated with the transport of the raw materials to the feed factory, electricity con- sumption by the feed factory in the production process, and dis- tribution of the produced poultry feed to the market. As per the information provided by the feed factory, the two mostly used heavy-duty trucks in the factory are "Ashok Leyland 1613" and "Tata SE 1613" with the load carrying capacity of 5-7 tonnes and have a maximum gross weight of 162,000 kg. The average mileage of these trucks was reported to be 4 km/L diesels. As most specifications are similar in the two trucks, we assumed similar emission patterns for both trucks.

Emissions from diesel engines depend on various aspects such as efficiency of engine, composition of the diesel fuel, operating temperature of engine, vehicle age and accumulated mileage, weather conditions etc. The procedure for estimating CO2 emission from vehicles is considerably different from the approach to esti- mate CH4 and N20 emissions. Emission of CO2 can be estimated by applying carbon content and a carbon oxidized factor to the consumed fuel quantity. In contrast, CH4 and N20 emissions largely depend on the emission control equipment used by the vehicle (e.g., type of catalytic converter) as well as vehicle's mileage. Be- sides, the emissions also depend on the engines' maintenance and operational practices. Corresponding to these complexities, a comparatively higher uncertainty level exists in terms of estimating CH4 and N20 emissions compared to CO2 emissions. We have used the emission factor for in-use heavy duty trucks as categorized by USEPA. The emission factors for a heavy duty truck are 0.0030 g of N20, 0.0032 g CH4 and 1.049 kg of CO2 per km [56,57].

a Chicks are brought to the farm and chicken are taken away from the farm once in 18 months in a batch, while eggs are transported once in a week mostly in the nearby market.
Table 5 Key parameters and assumption/estimation for energy and material (in and out) flows in the digester.

Description|Value|Unit Poultry litter entering the digester|0.3|tonne/day Human labour used in the digester|4|person-hours per day Amount of water required for mixing with litter|100|1/day Total solid presence in the poultry droppings|38|percentage Gas yield (litter and water mixing ratio @ 3:1)|0.132|m3/kg of total solid Amount of estimated biogas|20|m3/day Digested slurry coming out from the digesterb|0.378|tonne/day
a 1 person@8 h per day. b Biogas density is 1.158 kg biogas/Nm3 and with the assumption that no water loss in the process.

The required electricity to run the equipment in the feed factory such as the boiler, the cutting and mixing machine, the packing machine, light bulbs etc. is supplied by the national grid. The emission factor for the grid electricity production has been referred from the IEA [58]. The energy equivalent of human labour is esti- mated based on the 'Life-style Support Energy' as adopted by Khatiwada and Silveira [59]. Energy input from human labour can be computed by multiplying cost of labour by energy intensity of the economy. Minimum wage of a Bangladeshi labour is estimated $70 per month (@ 1$ = 75 taka). The per capita primary energy supply is 5.13 GJ and the per capita GNP is $ 772 [60]. Thus, energy value of labour is estimated to be 15.5 MJ/day. It is estimated that a labour works for 8 h a day. The estimated primary energy con- sumption mix for Bangladesh is biomass (62%), natural gas (25%), imported oil (12%) and imported coal (1%). Average emission factors for petroleum, coal and natural gas are considered to be 85 kg per GJ, 98 kg per GJ and 56 kg per GJ respectively [61]. The average primary energy consumption is 1.9 MJ per person-hour. Thus, with the above energy mix, the average emission is estimated at 0.047 kg CO2eq per person-hour. The emissions associated with the feed module are presented in Table 7.
30: Emissions from the feed module are mainly associated with the transport of the raw materials to the feed factory, electricity con- sumption by the feed factory in the production process, and dis- tribution of the produced poultry feed to the market. As per the information provided by the feed factory, the two mostly used heavy-duty trucks in the factory are "Ashok Leyland 1613" and "Tata SE 1613" with the load carrying capacity of 5-7 tonnes and have a maximum gross weight of 162,000 kg. The average mileage of these trucks was reported to be 4 km/L diesels. As most specifications are similar in the two trucks, we assumed similar emission patterns for both trucks.

Emissions from diesel engines depend on various aspects such as efficiency of engine, composition of the diesel fuel, operating temperature of engine, vehicle age and accumulated mileage, weather conditions etc. The procedure for estimating CO2 emission from vehicles is considerably different from the approach to esti- mate CH4 and N20 emissions. Emission of CO2 can be estimated by applying carbon content and a carbon oxidized factor to the consumed fuel quantity. In contrast, CH4 and N20 emissions largely depend on the emission control equipment used by the vehicle (e.g., type of catalytic converter) as well as vehicle's mileage. Be- sides, the emissions also depend on the engines' maintenance and operational practices. Corresponding to these complexities, a comparatively higher uncertainty level exists in terms of estimating CH4 and N20 emissions compared to CO2 emissions. We have used the emission factor for in-use heavy duty trucks as categorized by USEPA. The emission factors for a heavy duty truck are 0.0030 g of N20, 0.0032 g CH4 and 1.049 kg of CO2 per km [56,57].

processes. The amount of emissions is highly influenced by the diet composition, as well as the litter handling practices and environ- mental conditions. Chemical composition, pH value, biodegrad- ability, moisture, microbial populations and oxygen content of the litter can vary significantly depending upon litter handling prac- tices and environmental conditions [62].

3.2.2.1. Methane emissions. Poultry itself produces a very small amount of methane in its digestion process (enteric fermentation) as they have a simple mono-gastric digestive system [63]. There is no specific method suggested for estimating methane production through enteric fermentation [64]. However, a significant amount of methane is generated from the poultry farm due to the fermentation of organic matter presented in the litter. The quantity of methane generation depends upon the litter management practice i.e. how frequently the litter is removed from the farm, and how it is stored and handled [65]. Tier 1 method as suggested by IPCC [64] that requires the poultry population data and climate region and average temperature where the farm is situated, along with IPCC default emission factors, have been used in this study for GHG emissions estimation. Methane emission from manure/litter was estimated using the following equation as suggested by the IPCC [64].
VS: daily volatile solid litter from poultry, kg dry matter ani- mal day-1
Bo: maximum methane producing capacity or Biodegradability of litter (kg of CH4/kg VS)
MCF%: methane conversion factors for each manure/litter management system, depending on climate region.
N: Number of birds in the farm.
MS%: fraction of litter handled by storing in an open pit (1 if whole portion are handled by same method).

The values for the above parameters have been chosen for the Asia/Indian subcontinent climatic region and under the "warm" climate classification (average temperature of 25 °C). Biodegradability (Bo) of methane was assumed to be 0.24 kg of CH4/kg VS. For poultry litter stock in an open pit, the MCF value was found to be 65% [64]. The volatile solid (VS) presence in the litter was assumed to be 0.02 kg/day. The estimated methane emission from the pit storage litter was calculated at 1206 kgCO2eqPer 10000 eggs produced.

Litter management practices affect the emissions of CH4 significantly. If litter is handled wet (for example, collected in lagoon), it generates more CH4 (MCF = 79%) than if it is stored in a pit. Whereas if litter is handled as a dry solid, it decomposes under aerobic conditions, and production of CH4 (MCF = 1.5%) is small. Uncertainty in the emission associated with the litter handing could be around +30% [64]. This has been captured in the sensi- tivity analysis in section 5.

3.2.2.2. Nitrous oxide emissions. There will be direct and indirect Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions during the storage and treatment of manure. Nitrification and denitrification of nitrogen contained in the manure/litter causes the direct emissions. Nitrification is basi- cally the oxidation of ammonia nitrogen to nitrate nitrogen from stored poultry litter. Thus, sufficient supply of oxygen is required to form N20. Nitrification does not occur under anaerobic conditions. Denitrification is a process of natural decomposition of nitrates and nitrites present in the dinitrogen (N2).

Emissions associated with human labour are estimated from average fossil fuel consumption by agriculture labour.

The values for the above parameters have been chosen for the Asia/Indian subcontinent climatic region and under the "warm" climate classification (average temperature of 25 °C). Biodegradability (Bo) of methane was assumed to be 0.24 kg of CH4/kg VS. For poultry litter stock in an open pit, the MCF value was found to be 65% [64]. The volatile solid (VS) presence in the litter was assumed to be 0.02 kg/day. The estimated methane emission from the pit storage litter was calculated at 1206 kgCO2eqPer 10000 eggs produced.

Whereas if litter is handled as a dry solid, it decomposes under aerobic conditions, and production of CH4 (MCF = 1.5%) is small. Uncertainty in the emission associated with the litter handing could be around +30% [64]. This has been captured in the sensi- tivity analysis in section 5.

3.2.2.2. Nitrous oxide emissions. There will be direct and indirect Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions during the storage and treatment of manure. Nitrification and denitrification of nitrogen contained in the manure/litter causes the direct emissions. Nitrification is basi- cally the oxidation of ammonia nitrogen to nitrate nitrogen from stored poultry litter. Thus, sufficient supply of oxygen is required to form N20. Nitrification does not occur under anaerobic conditions. Denitrification is a process of natural decomposition of nitrates and nitrites present in the dinitrogen (N2).

39: N is the number of layer chicken in the farm. Nex is annual average Nitrogen excretion per chicken, kg N MS is the fraction of litter stockpiled (1 if all the litter is thrown outside). EF N20-D is the emission factor for direct N20 emissions from litter/manure management system (i.e. pit storage in this case) in kgN20-N/kg N.
Ammonia is one of the major emission problems found in a poultry farm, and it also produces bad odour. Elevated

concentrations of ammonia in the poultry farm may affect chicken's growth rate and reduce the egg production. Under such conditions, the chickens are more susceptible to diseases [66]. The ammonia concentration in the poultry farm could vary from 3.8 mg/m³ to 5.37 mg/m³ depending upon the poultry farm design and the litter handing practices [65,67]. Small increment in litter moisture can cause substantial increment in ammonia generation. Increment in the moisture from 20% to 30% can significantly raise the ammonia emission by 1.8 times at 24 °C [68]. Ammonia itself is not a greenhouse gas, but its deposition can add nitrogen to ecosystem, which can eventually create indirect nitrous oxide emissions. The indirect emission of N20 is due to the losses of volatile nitrogen largely in the form of ammonia and NOx from the manure. Such losses mainly depend on manure exposure time and, to some extent, on temperature but these are often very small in the poultry farm [13].

The value for annual average Nitrogen excretion is 0.6 kg N chicken-¹ yr. For pit storage litter/manure, the emission factor (EF N20-D) is found to be 0.002 kgN20-N/kg N [64]. Estimated annual direct nitrous oxide production is 1680 kgCO2eq- Therefore, the total nitrous oxide emission in terms of the functional unit is 39 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced (assuming indirect N20 emission to be 10% of direct emission).

The default emission factor (EF N20-D) for direct nitrous oxide varies significantly based on the type of litter handling system. In contrast to the CH4 emissions, if litter is handled wet in a lagoon, there is no N20 production (EF N20-D = 0). Whereas, if litter is handled as a dry solid, N20 production (EF N20-D = 0.02) could be 10 times higher compared to the pit storage.

3.2.2.3. Other emissions. Besides methane and nitrous oxide emis- sions, there are emissions associated with the use of energy in the poultry farm. Around 12.3 kWh of electricity is consumed in the poultry farm every day for operating electric fan, light bulbs and water pump. The electricity production in Bangladesh is dominated by natural gas (63%) with an average emission factor of 588 g of CO2eq/kWh [58]. So, the emissions resulting from the use of elec- tricity in the farm are 56 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced. The emission associated with human labour (32 person-hours/day) in the poultry farm is estimated at 12 kgCO2eqPer 10000 eggs produced. The emissions associated with the farm module are listed in Table 8.

43: <header>3.2.3. Emissions associated with the biogas module </header>

The analysis of the farm module showed a significant amount of GHG emissions when the litter is untreated and stored in an open pit. Integration of an anaerobic digester with the poultry farm helps in the treatment of the litter generated, and reduces emissions. In
Table 8 Emissions associated with the farm module, expressed in the functional unit.

Description|Value|Unit Emission associated with electricity consumed in the farm|56|kgCO2eqPer 10000 eggs produced Emission from human labour used in the farm|12|kgCO2eqPer 10000 eggs produced Emission from litter storage in a pit*|1245|kgCO2eqPer 10000 eggs produced Total emission from the farm module|1312|kgCO2eqPer 10000 eggs produced

"Following emission factors have been used for the emissions from manure/litter management IPCC [64]. Bo: maximum methane producing capacity or Biodegradability of litter = 0.24 kg of CH4/kg VS. MCF%: methane conversion factors for poultry litter stock in an open pit = 65%. Annual average Nitrogen excretion (N ex) = 0.6 kg N chicken The emission factor (EF N20-D) for pit storage Litter = 0.002 kgN20-N/kg N.

the digester, organic matter is degraded by the anaerobic micro- flora. The potential emissions in the biogas module are (i) emissions associated with construction of the digester (ii) leakage from the digester and, (iii) emissions from the slurry pit.

Bricks, brick chips, cement, sand and steel rods are commonly used in the construction of a digester. Other materials like paint and PVC pipes may be also used. Sand, brick, brick chips etc. are often locally available whereas cement, steel rod, gas valve and pipes are brought from outside. Estimation of emissions from all these ma- terials is complex as their manufacturing is spread over various places. For simplification, GHG emissions from cement, bricks and steel wire have been adopted from Josa et al. [69]. With the assumption of 25 year life span for an anaerobic digester [70], the estimated GHG emission associated with the construction of the digester is 2614.25 kgCO2eq i.e. 87.3 kgCO2eq per annum. This emission is very small (negligible) in comparison to the other sources of GHG emissions. Leakages are highly unpredictable as they directly depend upon the quality of the digester and its maintenance. The leakages can be up to 5% of the annually pro- duced biogas [49]. Such leakage can be avoided or minimized with leakage proof construction and with proper gas pipe connections.

The slurry coming out from the digester is another source of GHG emissions. It has been estimated that 378 kg of digested slurry is generated per day. The same equation (eq. (2)) used to estimate the methane emission from the litter can be used to estimate emission from the slurry pit. The methane conversion factor (MCF) in this case is assumed at 10%. Other values remaining the same, the estimated annual emission from the slurry in the pit of the poultry farm is estimated at 185.5 kg.CO2eqP 10000 eggs produced.

The use of digested slurry as bio-fertilizer has a positive impact on the environment as it reduces the overall GHG emissions resulting from synthetic fertilizers. The emission associated with synthetic fertilizers comprises two parts: the emissions related to the production of the synthetic fertilizer, and the emissions asso- ciated with application of the fertilizer. The nutrient content in digested poultry slurry is reported to be 1.84% of Nitrogen (N), 0.8% of Phosphorous (P) and Potash (K) is 0.42% whereas cow dung digested slurry content 1.2% of N, 0.6% of P and 0.4% of [71]. Hence, the poultry slurry is relatively better in terms of replacing synthetic fertilizers. The GHG emission factor in fertilizer production has been considered in previous studies (See Table 9) [72,73]. We adopted the average value of these emissions from Macedo et al. [72] and Nguyen et al. [73]. Khatiwada and Silveira [74] have also

48: Emission factors associated with synthetic fertilizer production.

Item|Emission factors (kgCO2eq/kg produced)|Emission factors (kgCO2eq/kg produced)|Emission factors (kgCO2eq/kg produced) Fertilizers|Macedo et al. [72]|Nguyen et al. [73]|Adopted average value Phosphorus|1.3|1.03|1.165 Nitrogen|3.97|3|3.485 Potash|0.71|0.69|0.7
adopted the average value in estimating emission factors associated with synthetic fertilizer in a case study of Nepal.

Synthetic fertilizer also has emissions associated with field application. Corresponding emissions from field application used in this analysis are listed in Table 10. Emissions associated with the human labour have not been considered in the analysis.

With these assumptions, if 50% of the digested slurry is applied to the agricultural field as fertilizer, 195 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs can be avoided as a result of substitution of synthetic fertilizers. Applications of digested slurry as soil fertilizer could have some environmental foot print due to GHG emissions mainly N20. However, there is still lack of clear understanding on the effect of digested slurry on microbial activity and conversion processes in soil, and thus its GHG balance and organic carbon sequestration [75]. In a recent study, Wang [76] has modeled the use of digested slurry as fertilizer which revealed that N20 emission is higher with lower ratio of Carbon to Nitrogen (C/N) and also increase with in- crease in amount of slurry used per hectare agriculture land. For the C/N ratio greater than 5, the emission is relatively very small and less sensitive to the variation in the amount of slurry applied per hectare of agriculture land compared to the synthetic fertilizer [76]. The C/N ratio for poultry litter is around 10 [23]. This N20 emission has not been accounted within this study. In any case, digested slurry is nutrient rich and definitely has high potentiality to be used as fertilizer in agriculture land displacing the synthetic fertilizers [76].

In addition, biogas offers an alternative to LPG as a clean cooking fuel, resulting in a reduction of GHG emissions when the substi- tution is made. The emission factors for biogas and LPG are shown in Table 11.

In contrast to fossil CO2 produced from LPG, CO2 emitted from biogas have shorter carbon cycle defined by atmospheric CO2 -plant -poultry birds -manures. Therefore, CO2 emissions from biogas are not considered to have GHG impact [13]. The avoided emissions using biogas as cooking fuel are estimated at 200 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced. The emissions associated with the biogas module are summarized in Table 12.
49: Emission factors associated with synthetic fertilizer production.

Item|Emission factors (kgCO2eq/kg produced)|Emission factors (kgCO2eq/kg produced)|Emission factors (kgCO2eq/kg produced) Fertilizers|Macedo et al. [72]|Nguyen et al. [73]|Adopted average value Phosphorus|1.3|1.03|1.165 Nitrogen|3.97|3|3.485 Potash|0.71|0.69|0.7
adopted the average value in estimating emission factors associated with synthetic fertilizer in a case study of Nepal.

Synthetic fertilizer also has emissions associated with field application. Corresponding emissions from field application used in this analysis are listed in Table 10. Emissions associated with the human labour have not been considered in the analysis.

With these assumptions, if 50% of the digested slurry is applied to the agricultural field as fertilizer, 195 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs can be avoided as a result of substitution of synthetic fertilizers. Applications of digested slurry as soil fertilizer could have some environmental foot print due to GHG emissions mainly N20. However, there is still lack of clear understanding on the effect of digested slurry on microbial activity and conversion processes in soil, and thus its GHG balance and organic carbon sequestration [75]. In a recent study, Wang [76] has modeled the use of digested slurry as fertilizer which revealed that N20 emission is higher with lower ratio of Carbon to Nitrogen (C/N) and also increase with in- crease in amount of slurry used per hectare agriculture land. For the C/N ratio greater than 5, the emission is relatively very small and less sensitive to the variation in the amount of slurry applied per hectare of agriculture land compared to the synthetic fertilizer [76]. The C/N ratio for poultry litter is around 10 [23]. This N20 emission has not been accounted within this study. In any case, digested slurry is nutrient rich and definitely has high potentiality to be used as fertilizer in agriculture land displacing the synthetic fertilizers [76].

In addition, biogas offers an alternative to LPG as a clean cooking fuel, resulting in a reduction of GHG emissions when the substi- tution is made. The emission factors for biogas and LPG are shown in Table 11.

In contrast to fossil CO2 produced from LPG, CO2 emitted from biogas have shorter carbon cycle defined by atmospheric CO2 -plant -poultry birds -manures. Therefore, CO2 emissions from biogas are not considered to have GHG impact [13]. The avoided emissions using biogas as cooking fuel are estimated at 200 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced. The emissions associated with the biogas module are summarized in Table 12.

51: <header>3.2.4. Emissions associated with the electricity module </header>

A biogas engine can be integrated with the biogas digester in the poultry farm to produce electricity. In this section, we estimate the GHG emission reduction potential with the integration of the electricity module in the poultry farm. The emissions associated with this module are mainly the emissions from biogas combustion in the engine for generating electricity. Emissions data from a small biogas engine was not available. We, therefore, adopted data for a biogas based Sterling engine (as a close proxy) which has a NOx emission of less than 0.21 g/kWh [77]. The average engine effi- ciency of a Sterling engine was assumed to be 33% regardless of the engine loading conditions. Thus, the estimated emission from combustion of biogas while generating 38 kWh of electricity/day is
Table 10 Emission factors associated with synthetic fertilizer application in the fields.

Table 11 Emission factors associated with cooking gas.
generating electricity can reduce the GHG emissions of poultry farms significantly (See Fig. 5).

The results from LCA are sensitive to the input parameters and assumption made. A clear understanding of the uncertainty and the assumptions used to generate the results is important in LCA. We have carried out a sensitivity analysis to verify how some param-

The avoided emissions are estimated at 171.5 kgCO2 per 10000 eggs produced. The emissions associated with the electricity module are summarized in Table 13.

54: <header>4. Emission from the poultry farm considering different cases and scenarios </header>
After analyzing the GHG emissions of individual modules, we can now consider the emissions generated in the different cases described in Table 1. The results are shown in Table 14 and Fig. 5.

The poultry (layer chicken) farm generates 1735 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced, emissions that are mainly associated with poultry litter disposal (Case 1). The emissions can be significantly reduced by introducing an anaerobic digester to treat the poultry litter. In this case (Case 2), emissions can be reduced to 612 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced. The emission can be further reduced to 324 kgCO2 per 10000 eggs if 50% of the digested slurry is utilized as bio-fertilizer in agriculture (Case 3). If the biogas is used for producing electricity, the emission will be reduced to 408 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced (Case 4). This shows that managing and utilizing the poultry litter to generate biogas for cooking or

eters influence the amount of GHG emissions in the poultry farm. Two parameters affecting emissions in the farm are the methane emission factor (MCF) and the portion of the slurry that is used as fertilizer in the agriculture fields.

As mentioned earlier, depending upon how litter is managed, the moisture content in the litter may vary, and consequently also the methane conversion factors (from MCF = 1.5% for dry solid litter management system to MCF = 79% for wet litters handling in la- goons at ambient temperature of 25 °C). This has significant in- fluence on CH4 emissions [78]. The ambient temperature also has significant impact on the methane emission factor especially when the litter is managed in wet condition. With increase in the tem- perature, the MCF values may go up. Uncertainty in the emissions associated with the litter handing could be +30% [64].

Fig. 6 shows that the variation in MCF has significant impact on GHG emissions, for example, in the 1st case in which litter is not treated and stocked in pit. With a variation of +30% in MCF, there will be a variation of +21% GHG emissions in the first case. Thus, proper management of litter is vital to reduce emissions. In addi- tion, reducing odour and ammonia concentration in the farm is important. Regular cleaning, maintaining the litter dry and covering with some plastic during short-time storage are some of the methods that can be used for minimizing the odour and ammonia, and also methane emissions [79]. Integration of anaer- obic digester in the rural poultry farm can reduce the odour emissions by 70-95% [26]. Many rural poultry farmers in

Bangladesh are not aware of these issues and, as a consequence, litter management practices are often very poor [10].

The market for digested slurry to be used as fertilizer is subject to some degree of uncertainty, which can have a significant impact on the GHG emission results. Sensitivity analysis with slurry utili- zation has been performed in two cases (i) the poultry farm with biogas digester (Case 3) and, (ii) the poultry farm with electricity generation (Case 4). The analysis shows that the utilization of digested slurry as bio fertilizer is important for reducing the GHG emissions. If 100% slurry is utilized, the emissions are reduced by 17 times in case 3 and by 6 times in case 4 as compared to a situation without slurry utilization (see Fig. 7).

The study adopted LCA to evaluate the potential GHG emissions from poultry farm considering the local supply chains and analyse the pathways in reducing the emissions from the poultry farm. In addition, there are some other factors influencing the choice of the

pathways which includes investment cost, social acceptance, socio- economic benefits, and existing policy regulations which cannot be covered by LCAs [80]. Further expansion of the research on the above aspects along with LCA will contribute to decision-making in the alternative pathways [81]. However, previous studies have shown that integrating biogas unit or electricity generation unit with the livestock farm is financially attractive and socially highly acceptable as it reduces the odour from the poultry farm and pro- duces clean energy [21-23]. This study is still highly significant in enhancing the understanding of the environmental impacts asso- ciated with the rural poultry farm and evaluating the environ- mental benefits of adopting various pathways. This is a step forward in motivating the policy makers and planners to think further in this direction.

In addi- tion, reducing odour and ammonia concentration in the farm is important. Regular cleaning, maintaining the litter dry and covering with some plastic during short-time storage are some of the methods that can be used for minimizing the odour and ammonia, and also methane emissions [79]. Integration of anaer- obic digester in the rural poultry farm can reduce the odour emissions by 70-95% [26]. Many rural poultry farmers in

58: <header>4. Emission from the poultry farm considering different cases and scenarios </header>
After analyzing the GHG emissions of individual modules, we can now consider the emissions generated in the different cases described in Table 1. The results are shown in Table 14 and Fig. 5.

The poultry (layer chicken) farm generates 1735 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced, emissions that are mainly associated with poultry litter disposal (Case 1). The emissions can be significantly reduced by introducing an anaerobic digester to treat the poultry litter. In this case (Case 2), emissions can be reduced to 612 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced. The emission can be further reduced to 324 kgCO2 per 10000 eggs if 50% of the digested slurry is utilized as bio-fertilizer in agriculture (Case 3). If the biogas is used for producing electricity, the emission will be reduced to 408 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs produced (Case 4). This shows that managing and utilizing the poultry litter to generate biogas for cooking or

eters influence the amount of GHG emissions in the poultry farm. Two parameters affecting emissions in the farm are the methane emission factor (MCF) and the portion of the slurry that is used as fertilizer in the agriculture fields.

As mentioned earlier, depending upon how litter is managed, the moisture content in the litter may vary, and consequently also the methane conversion factors (from MCF = 1.5% for dry solid litter management system to MCF = 79% for wet litters handling in la- goons at ambient temperature of 25 °C). This has significant in- fluence on CH4 emissions [78]. The ambient temperature also has significant impact on the methane emission factor especially when the litter is managed in wet condition. With increase in the tem- perature, the MCF values may go up. Uncertainty in the emissions associated with the litter handing could be +30% [64].

Fig. 6 shows that the variation in MCF has significant impact on GHG emissions, for example, in the 1st case in which litter is not treated and stocked in pit. With a variation of +30% in MCF, there will be a variation of +21% GHG emissions in the first case. Thus, proper management of litter is vital to reduce emissions. In addi- tion, reducing odour and ammonia concentration in the farm is important. Regular cleaning, maintaining the litter dry and covering with some plastic during short-time storage are some of the methods that can be used for minimizing the odour and ammonia, and also methane emissions [79]. Integration of anaer- obic digester in the rural poultry farm can reduce the odour emissions by 70-95% [26]. Many rural poultry farmers in

60: This paper has estimated GHG emissions from a poultry farm in Bangladesh using LCA. We analyzed the emissions reduction po- tential when poultry litter is treated through an anaerobic digestion and the gas is used (i) to provide cooking gas or (ii) to generate electricity. The analysis showed that the emission intensity of the poultry (layer chicken) farm studied is 1735 kgCO 2eq per 10000 eggs produced. On-farm activities have major impact on GHG emissions among the various activities analyzed in the poultry supply chain.

The methodological differences, system boundaries and alloca- tion approach used for an LCA analysis may vary from case to case, in addition to the specific context of production conditions. Here, we have shown the conditions and emission reduction potential in the context of poultry farms of Bangladesh. In Iran, GHG emissions for a layer poultry farm with 1000 chickens were calculated at 1956 kgCO2eq per 10000 eggs using LCA, which is higher than our case [82,83]. Our estimate is within the emission intensity range for

Fig. 7. GHG emission variation with slurry utilization in different cases. Note: Case 3: Biogas plant using litter, digested slurry utilized as fertilizer. Case 4: Biogas plant supplying electricity, and digested slurry utilized as fertilizer.

layer chicken farms estimated by MacLeod et al. [27] for South Asia, temperate region. But this difference observed in emission in- tensity indicates that caution is needed when calculating the ben- efits of emissions reduction actions. Treating the poultry litter in a biogas digester can substantially reduce the GHG emissions while also providing energy. The emis- sion intensity is reduced by 65% if the litter produced in the farm is managed using anaerobic digester, and the biogas produced is used to replace LPG for cooking purposes. The emission intensity can be reduced by 81%, if 50% of the digested slurry is used as bio-fertilizer in agriculture. The emission intensity will be reduced by 76%, if biogas is used for generating electricity and 50% of the digested slurry is used as bio-fertilizer. If 100% of the digested slurry is used as bio fertilizer, the total GHG emissions could be reduced by 17 times compared to a situation without slurry utilization. The sensitivity analysis revealed that the emissions associated with poultry farm largely depend on how the litter is handled in the farm. Since production of such emissions is of microbial nature, maintaining the litter dry in the poultry farm helps to reduce the emissions of ammonia and methane. This is advantageous for reducing health risks, odour and climate impact. This study has helped to identify the major sources of emissions in a traditional layer chicken farm in Bangladesh. The analysis shows that the use of poultry litter from such farms can serve not only to generate energy and produce bio-fertilizer, but also to reduce GHG emissions from a growing industry. Improving the conditions of poultry farms in Bangladesh should, therefore, be seriously considered as part of comprehensive programs to pro- mote renewable energy production from litter, create markets for bio-fertilizers, and mitigate GHG emissions. Such programs are likely to be eligible for climate funds under the UNFCCC. Increasing energy costs, growing environmental awareness and the evolving carbon credit market could also stimulate rural poultry sector to install anaerobic digester for managing poultry litter, generating energy and reducing GHG emissions.
